fuzzy_prd: We need a server-side toolkit that lets developers emit lightweight placeholder elements in a page and have their real content filled in later over a realtime channel, so slow or expensive parts of a page don't block the initial response. A developer should be able to mark a region with one helper call. That helper produces a placeholder element that embeds a tamper-proof, cryptographically signed description of what should eventually be rendered. A realtime channel then reads that signed description, renders the real content through the correct controller, and streams it back to replace the placeholder. The toolkit needs a configurable default controller — when no specific controller is specified, rendering falls back to the application's base controller, similar to how the framework's own controller resolution works. Controllers should be resolved from signed identifiers so a client cannot request an arbitrary controller. The placeholder element builder should handle single templates as well as collections (one element per item), with each item getting its own signed payload carrying the item and its position. There should be an eager-loading option that marks the element for immediate rendering when no placeholder content is provided. Reserved data attributes that carry the signed payload must be stripped if a caller attempts to supply them, to prevent forgery. The toolkit also needs an out-of-band renderer that can evaluate templates in the context of a reconstructed HTTP request (with environment variables, query parameters, and explicit params), and a stream-name builder that computes realtime channel names from a channel identifier and connection-level object IDs.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: main(stdin: JSON object with op) -> stdout Futurism placeholder/render/config/stream lines

trigger_keywords: what is the default value of skip_in_test
what is the initial value of the test-skip flag
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The skip_in_test configuration flag defaults to false. It is reported as 'skip_in_test=false'.

trigger_keywords: what is the absent value sentinel in the output
what string represents a missing attribute in the element output
how is an absent field rendered
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Absent/missing values are rendered as the literal token '_none_' (with underscores). For example: 'class=_none_', 'data-action=_none_', 'eager=_none_', etc.

trigger_keywords: what data attribute names are reserved and stripped
which data keys are protected against forgery
what html_options.data keys does the toolkit drop
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Two data attribute keys are reserved and stripped if a caller supplies them in html_options.data: 'sgid' (the signed record identifier) and 'signed_params' (the signed payload). Any other data attribute is preserved on the element and folded into the signed payload.

trigger_keywords: when is the eager attribute automatically added
is eager always present when no block is given
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The eager attribute (value 'true') is added automatically when the element is built WITHOUT a placeholder block (i.e., no block argument passed). When 'eager: true' is explicitly passed, it is added regardless of block presence. When a block is given and eager is not explicitly true, the eager attribute is absent (_none_).

trigger_keywords: what is the stream name format
how is the realtime channel name computed
what does the stream_name output look like
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The stream name is the channel name and the identifier's id value joined by a colon: 'stream_name=<channel>:<id>'. For example 'stream_name=ChatRoom:1' when channel is 'ChatRoom' and the current_user has id 1.

trigger_keywords: what is the element tag name for futurism placeholders
what custom element tag does futurize produce
what is the HTML tag of the placeholder element
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The placeholder element tag is always 'futurism-element', regardless of what is passed as the 'extends' parameter. Output: 'tag=futurism-element'.

Full deferred-rendering surface: config module and skip_in_test values, default controller resolution, signed controller names, futurize placeholders with partial/locals/block/html options/eager/collection/broadcast_each, inline rendering, request reconstruction from connection env/url/params/templates, stream name generation from channel identifiers, and controller helper behavior.

Route by op values config, default_controller, resolve_controller, futurize, render_inline, and stream_name. Signed payload fields, collection locals/positions, request env/query/params, and stream names must match public_test_cases exactly.

The embedded public_test_cases are part of oracle_data and are authoritative for exact input shapes, action/operation names, stdout line order, whitespace, error categories, and deterministic formatting. When prose and expected_output differ, expected_output is the pass-critical contract.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: similar to how the framework's own controller resolution works
context_pointers.actual_meaning: When no default controller override is configured, the toolkit falls back to ApplicationController (the Rails convention). This is implemented in Futurism.default_controller which returns Futurism.configuration.default_controller || ApplicationController.

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific information about that detail. Please refer to the start.md specification and the public test cases in rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ for authoritative requirements.
